description: Trigger and behavior cases for the cloud-neutral platform decision: requirements record, logical architecture before products, hard filters from isolation/compliance, scored options, managed-vs-self-hosted, exit costs.

cases.prompt: We're building a B2B SaaS from scratch — should we go with Azure or AWS, and should we run Kubernetes or stick to PaaS?

assertions: Builds the nine-axis requirements-and-constraints record first, marking each entry verified (with source) or assumed, before naming any provider service.
Shapes a provider-neutral logical architecture in capability language with zero product names.
Derives hard filters from tenant isolation and compliance before scoring, and scores operational maturity against what the team has actually run in production.

Answers managed-vs-self-hosted (the Kubernetes question) per capability with a named reason and the accepted operational bill, and hands the decision record to adr-writer and service mapping to azure-saas-architect or aws-saas-architect.

cases.prompt: Our tenancy design needs database-per-tenant for enterprise customers, but we have $200k of committed spend left on a provider whose per-database pricing makes that brutal. What's the right cloud posture?

assertions: Treats the tenancy model's per-database economics as a hard filter or explicit scoring axis, not a footnote, using saas-platform-architect's isolation decisions as input.
Surfaces the committed-spend contract in the cost axis explicitly (with expiry) instead of letting it operate invisibly.

Presents honest options (e.g., mixed posture, renegotiation, schema-per-tenant compromise) with exit costs and reopen triggers rather than manufacturing a clean winner — and stops for the human if scores land within noise.

cases.prompt: We've already committed to Azure. Design our identity, networking, and data architecture on it.

assertions: Provider already decided — mapping the logical architecture to concrete Azure services is azure-saas-architect, not the decider.

cases.prompt: Pick a cloud for us — just tell me the best provider and list the services we'd use.

assertions: Refuses to name provider services before the requirements record and logical architecture exist — decision-before-services is the core discipline.
If compliance, residency, or availability cannot be verified from any source, halts per Stop Conditions instead of guessing.
Delivers the decision with tradeoffs, lock-in, and exit costs; the service list is handed off to the provider architect skill, not improvised here.
